section.title: A Time for Everything
section.sectionRationale: After chasing pleasure, work, and wisdom and finding them all vapor, the Teacher lifts his eyes to the rhythm of the seasons. Everything has its appointed time, set by a God who works beyond our sight. The chapter moves from the famous poem of the times, through the confession that God has made everything beautiful in its time and set eternity in the human heart, to the sobering truth that man and beast share one breath and return to one dust. The answer under the sun is small but real: fear God, and receive food, work, and joy from His hand.
section.thesis: God has fixed a time for everything and does all things well, yet He hides the whole from us and sets eternity in our hearts, so that we cannot master life but are driven to fear Him and to receive daily bread, work, and joy as His gift.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the poem of the times, a season for every purpose under heaven; the burden and the gift, God's work endures and He has set eternity in the heart; and from dust to dust, where judgment, death, and the shared breath of man and beast press the reader back to God.

verses.text: To everything there is a {{zeman|season}}, and a {{eth|time}} for every purpose under heaven:
a time to be born and a time to die, a time to plant and a time to uproot,
a time to kill and a time to heal, a time to break down and a time to build,
a time to weep and a time to laugh, a time to mourn and a time to dance,
a time to cast away stones and a time to gather stones together, a time to embrace and a time to refrain from embracing,
a time to search and a time to count as lost, a time to keep and a time to discard,
a time to tear and a time to mend, a time to be silent and a time to speak,
a time to love and a time to hate, a time for war and a time for peace.
What does the worker gain from his toil?
I have seen the burden that God has laid upon the sons of men to occupy them.
He has made everything beautiful in its time. He has also set {{olam|eternity}} in the hearts of men, yet they cannot fathom the work that God has done from beginning to end.
I know that there is nothing better for them than to rejoice and do good while they live,
and also that every man should eat and drink and find satisfaction in all his labor, this is the gift of God.
I know that everything God does endures forever; nothing can be added to it or taken from it. God does it so that they should fear Him.
What exists has already been, and what will be has already been, for God will call to account what has passed.

units.label: From dust to dust
units.note: one breath for man and beast

verses.text: Furthermore, I saw under the sun that in the place of judgment there is wickedness, and in the place of righteousness there is wickedness.
I said in my heart, “God will judge the righteous and the wicked, since there is a time for every activity and every deed.”
I said to myself, “As for the sons of men, God tests them so that they may see for themselves that they are but beasts.”

For the fates of both men and beasts are the same: As one dies, so dies the other, they all have the same {{ruach|breath}}. Man has no advantage over the animals, since everything is {{hevel|futile}}.
All go to one place; all come from dust, and all return to dust.
Who knows if the {{ruach|spirit}} of man rises upward and the spirit of the animal descends into the earth?
I have seen that there is nothing better for a man than to enjoy his work, because that is his lot. For who can bring him to see what will come after him?

zeman.gloss: An appointed season or fixed time. Paired here with eth, it opens the poem: every purpose under heaven has a season already set by God, not left to chance or to human control.

eth.gloss: A time, an occasion, the right moment. It drums through the poem twenty eight times, a time to be born, a time to die, insisting that the moments of a life fall within times that God has appointed.

olam.gloss: Eternity, the far distance of time past and future, the everlasting. God has set olam in the human heart: a sense of forever, a longing that outruns this life, which nothing under the sun can satisfy.

ruach.gloss: Breath, wind, or spirit. Man and beast share one ruach and return to dust; yet the same word opens the question of whether the spirit of man rises upward, a hope the whole canon answers in the resurrection.

hevel.gloss: Vapor, breath, mist. The keyword of the book, rendered futile, meaningless, or fleeting. It names how life under the sun slips through the fingers like breath on a cold morning, here because man, like the beast, dies and returns to dust.

basic.context: Having tried pleasure, work, and wisdom and found them all like vapor, the Teacher steps back to look at the shape of time itself. The famous poem in verses 1 to 8 says that everything, birth and death, weeping and laughing, war and peace, has its appointed season. Then he asks the honest question: if God has set all these times, what do we actually gain by our toil? His answer is humbling. God has made everything beautiful in its time and has put a sense of eternity in our hearts, yet He does not let us see the whole picture. So the best thing is to fear God and receive food, work, and joy as gifts from His hand, even though man and beast alike return to the dust.
basic.hermeneutics: The poem is not fatalism, as if we were trapped in a machine. It is a wisdom picture of a world ordered by a sovereign God, meant to teach humility and trust rather than despair. The key question is what verse 11 means by eternity in the heart: God has planted in us a longing for what is forever, which is why nothing temporary ever fully satisfies. The darkness of verses 18 to 21, that we share one breath with the animals and go to the same dust, is real and is meant to drive us to God, not to numb us.
basic.originalLanguages: The poem hammers one word, eth, time, and pairs it with zeman, season: God has fixed the times. In verse 11 God sets olam, eternity, in the heart, a longing for forever that this world cannot fill. In verses 19 and 21 the word ruach, breath or spirit, links man and beast, both die and return to dust, yet ruach also opens the question of whether the spirit of man rises upward. And the summary word of the book, hevel, vapor, names how fleeting all of this is under the sun.
basic.typology: The eternity God sets in the heart is a God shaped ache that only the eternal Son can fill. The time to be born and a time to die finds its answer in the One who was born to die and then rose, breaking the grip of the dust. The judgment the Teacher expects, God will judge the righteous and the wicked, points to the day when Christ will judge the living and the dead.
basic.christ: The longing for eternity that nothing under the sun can satisfy is met in Jesus, who offers eternal life. The dust to dust verdict of verse 20 is exactly what Christ entered and overturned: He was made subject to futility, died, and rose, so that the spirit of man rising upward is no longer only a question but a hope secured. The One who makes everything beautiful in its time made the fullness of time arrive in Christ.
basic.god: God is sovereign over every season; He makes everything beautiful in its time, and what He does endures forever. He is generous, giving food, drink, work, and joy as gifts. He is also hidden, refusing to let us fathom His work from beginning to end, so that we would fear Him rather than master Him. And He is the just Judge who will call every deed to account.
basic.crossReferences: Romans 8 says creation was subjected to futility in hope, and will be set free. Psalm 90 pleads teach us to number our days. Acts 17 says God has determined appointed times. John 3 and 17 answer the ache for eternity with eternal life in Christ.
basic.oneStory: This is the honest verdict of life under the sun: God orders every season, but we cannot see the whole, and we return to the dust like the beasts. The ache for eternity He plants in us is answered only outside the sun, in the God who made time and then entered it in Christ to conquer death, so that the longing for forever finds its home.

context.literary: The chapter opens with a tightly patterned poem, fourteen pairs of opposites framed by a time to and a time to, a merism that gathers up the whole of life between its poles. From the poem the Teacher turns to reflection, verses 9 to 15, on God's enduring work and the gift of enjoyment, then to a darker meditation, verses 16 to 22, on injustice, death, and the shared fate of man and beast. The refrains under the sun and nothing better bind it to the rest of the book.
context.historical: Ecclesiastes reflects on life in a settled, prosperous world where wealth and labor could not answer the questions of death and meaning. Its wisdom stands in the tradition of Israel's sages, but it presses harder than Proverbs on the limits of what wisdom can secure, insisting that only the fear of God gives a foothold.
context.canonical: The poem of the times reads the created order that Genesis 1 set in motion, with its evenings and mornings and seasons. The verdict of dust to dust echoes Genesis 3:19, and the ache for eternity looks ahead to the eternal life promised in the Gospels and secured in the resurrection. Ecclesiastes keeps the canon honest about death while pointing beyond the sun for the answer.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To humble the reader before a God who orders all times and yet hides the whole from us, so that we stop trying to master life, learn to fear God, and receive daily bread, work, and joy as His gift, even under the shadow of death.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The poem and the meditations largely describe the world as it is under the sun, the seasons, the injustice, the death we share with the beasts. The prescriptions are few and clear: fear God, verse 14; do good and rejoice while you live, verse 12; and enjoy your work as your God given lot, verses 13 and 22.

debates.question: Does 3:1-8 teach fatalism, or the sovereign ordering of times that calls for trust?

views.label: A counsel of fatalism
views.case: Read on its own, the relentless list of fixed times can sound deterministic, as if human effort changes nothing and we are simply swept along by forces beyond us, which fuels the Teacher's question in verse 9, what does the worker gain.

views.label: Sovereign ordering that calls for trust
views.case: The wider chapter says God makes everything beautiful in its time and does all things well, so the fixed times are not a blind machine but the wise governance of a good God, and the right response is humble trust and enjoyment, not resignation.

debates.bearing: The book itself tips toward the second reading: because a wise God appoints the seasons, we can neither control life nor despair of it, but fear Him and receive each day as gift.

debates.question: What does it mean that God has set eternity in the heart, 3:11?

views.label: A sense of the everlasting and a longing for it
views.case: God has planted in us an awareness of forever, so that we cannot be satisfied by anything temporary; the aching restlessness of the human heart is evidence that we were made for more than life under the sun.

views.label: A sense of the whole scope of time we cannot grasp
views.case: The same clause is paired with the fact that we cannot fathom God's work from beginning to end, so olam may stress that we glimpse the vast sweep of time yet cannot master it, which drives us to trust the God who can.

debates.bearing: Both readings press the same way: the eternity in our hearts outruns what this world can give, and only the eternal God, revealed in Christ, can satisfy it.

study.originalLanguages: The poem is built on the word eth, time, the right moment, repeated in fourteen matched pairs and opened by zeman, appointed season: God has set the times of a life. In verse 11 God places olam, eternity, in the human heart, a sense of the everlasting that no passing thing can fill, even as He withholds the full view of His work. In verses 19 and 21 ruach, breath or spirit, joins man and beast in death and dust, yet the same word raises the question of whether the spirit of man ascends. Over it all hangs hevel, vapor: life under the sun is as fleeting as breath.

typology.type: Eternity set in the human heart, 3:11
typology.fulfillment: The God shaped longing for forever, met only in Christ who gives eternal life, John 17:3.

typology.type: A time to be born and a time to die, 3:2
typology.fulfillment: Jesus, born in the fullness of time and dying at the appointed hour, who then conquers death, Galatians 4:4.

typology.type: God will judge the righteous and the wicked, 3:17
typology.fulfillment: The final judgment entrusted to Christ, who will judge the living and the dead, Acts 17:31.

study.christ: The ache for eternity that nothing under the sun can satisfy is answered in Jesus, who says this is eternal life, to know the true God and the One He has sent. The verdict of dust to dust, the shared breath of man and beast, is the futility that Christ entered: He was born to die, tasted the dust of the grave, and rose, so that the open question of verse 21, whether the spirit of man rises upward, becomes settled hope. He is the One in whom the fullness of time came, who makes all things beautiful in their time, and to whom all judgment is given.
study.god: God is the sovereign Lord of every season, who makes everything beautiful in its time and whose work endures forever, beyond adding or subtracting. He is generous, handing out food, drink, labor, and joy as gifts to be received. He is deliberately hidden, keeping the whole of His work from our sight so that we fear Him rather than presume to master Him. And He is the just Judge who will call every deed, righteous and wicked, to account.

crossReferences.note: For dust you are, and to dust you shall return, the verdict the Teacher restates over man and beast alike.
Creation was subjected to futility in hope, and will be set free into the glory of the children of God.
Teach us to number our days, that we may present a heart of wisdom, the prayer this chapter provokes.
God determined appointed times and has fixed a day when He will judge the world through the risen Christ.

study.oneStory: Ecclesiastes 3 gives the honest ledger of life under the sun: a God who orders every season, a heart aching for an eternity it cannot reach, and a body destined for the dust. The Teacher can only counsel fear of God and the humble enjoyment of daily gifts. The rest of the canon supplies what he could not see from under the sun: the eternal God stepped into time in Christ, entered the dust of death, and rose, so that the longing for forever is met and the judgment he awaited is placed in nail pierced hands.

quiz.q: What is the main point of the poem in 3:1-8?

options.text: God has appointed a season for every purpose, so life's times are under His sovereign ordering.
options.note: The repeated a time to underlines that God, not chance, sets the seasons of life.

options.text: Life is a series of opposite experiences that balance each other out.
options.note: The pattern of opposites is real, but the deeper point is that God appoints each time.

options.text: We are trapped by fate and our efforts change nothing.
options.note: The chapter reads the times as God's wise ordering, not a blind machine.

options.text: We should try to control the timing of everything ourselves.
options.note: The poem teaches the opposite: the times belong to God, not to us.

quiz.q: What does it mean that God has set eternity in the human heart, 3:11?

options.text: God has planted a longing for the everlasting that nothing temporary can satisfy.
options.note: The ache for forever is why life under the sun never fully fills us.

options.text: God has given us a sense of the vast sweep of time we cannot fully grasp.
options.note: A fair reading, and it points the same way, toward trusting the God who sees the whole.

options.text: God has made us able to figure out His plans from beginning to end.
options.note: The verse says the opposite, that we cannot fathom His work.

options.text: God has promised everyone eternal life regardless of anything.
options.note: The verse names a longing, not a blanket promise; that longing is met in Christ.

quiz.q: What does the Teacher commend as the good response within this life?

options.text: To fear God and to receive food, work, and joy as gifts from His hand.
options.note: Verses 12 to 14 and 22 hold together fearing God and enjoying His gifts.

options.text: To eat, drink, and enjoy one's work as a God given lot.
options.note: True, but it leaves out the anchor, the fear of God in verse 14.

options.text: To work harder so that our toil finally pays off.
options.note: The chapter questions what toil gains and points instead to gift and to fearing God.

options.text: To stop caring about anything since all is fleeting.
options.note: The Teacher counsels reverent joy, not numb indifference.

quiz.q: Why does the Teacher dwell on man and beast sharing one breath and one dust, 3:18-21?

options.text: To humble us before death and drive us to fear God rather than trust ourselves.
options.note: The shared dust exposes our frailty and presses us back to God.

options.text: To show that human life is fragile and ends like every other creature's.
options.note: True as far as it goes, but the aim is to humble us toward God, not merely to observe frailty.

options.text: To prove that there is no difference at all between people and animals.
options.note: He raises the question of the spirit rising upward, hinting at a real difference and a hope.

options.text: To teach that death is the final word and nothing lies beyond.
options.note: The canon answers his open question with resurrection hope in Christ.

quiz.q: How does this chapter point forward to Christ?

options.text: The eternity in the heart and the dust of death are answered by Christ, who gives eternal life and conquers the grave.
options.note: The ache for forever and the verdict of dust both find their answer in the risen Christ.

options.text: It shows a longing for something more that only God can satisfy.
options.note: True, and that longing is met specifically in Jesus and His resurrection.

options.text: It promises that hard work will always be rewarded in this life.
options.note: The chapter questions the gain of toil and points beyond the sun for the answer.

options.text: It has nothing to do with Christ or the wider Bible.
options.note: The New Testament takes up its themes of futility, eternity, and judgment in Christ.

questions: The poem says there is a time for everything. What season do you sense you are in right now, and how would it change things to entrust its timing to God?
God has set eternity in your heart, a longing nothing temporary can fill. Where have you been trying to satisfy that ache with things that were only ever passing?
The Teacher commends receiving food, work, and joy as gifts from God's hand. What ordinary gift might you have been treating as an achievement rather than receiving with thanks?
God keeps the whole of His work hidden so that we would fear Him. Where do you need to trade the craving to understand everything for simple trust in Him?
Man and beast share the dust, yet Christ has conquered the grave. How does the resurrection change the way you face your own mortality this week?

translationNotes.note: Season translates zeman, an appointed time, and time translates eth, the fitting moment; together they stress that the occasions of life are set by God, not by chance.
Eternity translates olam, the everlasting or the far reach of time; God sets it in the heart, giving us a longing for forever that this world cannot satisfy.
The same word ruach means both breath and spirit; man and beast share one ruach and return to dust, while verse 21 uses it to raise the hope that the spirit of man rises upward.
Futile translates hevel, literally vapor or breath, the book's keyword for what is fleeting and cannot be grasped, not that life is worthless but that it slips away under the sun.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Ecclesiastes 3:1-22. Poetic lines rendered as flowing prose within each verse. All em and en dashes converted to commas; no em or en dashes used.
